content:
# Mark Chapter 14 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), folios 39v–40v (PDF pages 79–81) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/greek-NT/MRK_014_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/greek-NT/MRK_014_page_002.jpg`, `source-texts/greek-NT/MRK_014_page_003.jpg`
**Secondary Witness:** Codex Vaticanus (B, 03) — consulted via [DigiVatLib](https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209)

---

¹ It was two days before Passover and the Festival of Unleavened Bread. The chief priests and the scholars were looking for a sneaky way to arrest Jesus and kill him.

² "Not during the festival," they said. "The people might riot."

³ While Jesus was in Bethany, eating at the home of Simon the leper, a woman came in carrying an alabaster jar of very expensive perfume — pure nard. She broke the jar open and poured the perfume over his head.

⁴ Some of those present were outraged. "What a waste!" they said to each other.

⁵ "That perfume was worth a year's wages! It could have been sold and the money given to the poor." And they berated her harshly.

⁶ But Jesus said, "Leave her alone. Why are you giving her a hard time? She's done something beautiful for me.

⁷ You will always have the poor among you, and you can help them whenever you want. But you won't always have me.

⁸ She did what she could — she anointed my body for burial ahead of time.

⁹ I tell you the truth: wherever the good news is proclaimed throughout the world, what this woman has done will also be told, in her memory."

¹⁰ Then Judas Iscariot — one of the twelve — went to the chief priests to hand Jesus over to them.

¹¹ They were thrilled and promised to pay him. So Judas began watching for the right moment to betray him.

¹² On the first day of the Festival of Unleavened Bread, when the Passover lamb was being sacrificed, his disciples asked, "Where do you want us to go and prepare the Passover meal for you?"

¹³ He sent two of his disciples ahead with these instructions: "Go into the city. A man carrying a water jar will meet you — follow him.

¹⁴ When he enters a house, tell the owner, 'The Teacher asks: Where is the guest room where I can eat the Passover with my disciples?'

¹⁵ He'll show you a large upstairs room, fully furnished and ready. Make preparations there."

¹⁶ The disciples went into the city and found everything exactly as Jesus had described. They prepared the Passover meal.

¹⁷ That evening, Jesus arrived with the twelve.

¹⁸ While they were reclining at the table eating, Jesus said, "I tell you the truth: one of you eating with me right now is going to betray me."

¹⁹ They were devastated. One after another they asked him, "It's not me, is it?"

²⁰ He said, "It's one of the twelve — someone who is dipping bread into the bowl with me at this very moment.

²¹ The Son of Man will go to his fate, just as Scripture says. But how terrible it will be for the one who betrays him. It would be better for that man if he had never been born."

²² During the meal, Jesus took bread, blessed it, broke it, and gave it to them. "Take it," he said. "This is my body."

²³ Then he took a cup of wine, gave thanks to God, and passed it to them. They all drank from it.

²⁴ He said, "This is my blood of the covenant, poured out for many.

²⁵ I tell you the truth: I will not drink wine again until the day I drink it new in God's kingdom."

²⁶ After singing a hymn, they walked out to the Mount of Olives.

²⁷ Jesus told them, "All of you will fall away, because Scripture says, 'I will strike the shepherd, and the sheep will scatter.'

²⁸ But after I've been raised, I'll go ahead of you to Galilee."

²⁹ Peter declared, "Even if everyone else falls away, I never will."

³⁰ Jesus said to him, "I tell you the truth: today — this very night — before the rooster crows twice, you will disown me three times."

³¹ Peter insisted even more forcefully, "Even if I have to die with you, I will never disown you!" And all the others said the same thing.

³² They came to a place called Gethsemane. Jesus told his disciples, "Sit here while I pray."

³³ He took Peter, James, and John along with him. A wave of anguish and dread swept over him.

³⁴ "My soul is crushed with grief," he told them. "I feel as if I'm dying. Stay here and keep watch."

³⁵ He walked a little farther, collapsed to the ground, and prayed that if there were any way, this hour might pass him by.

³⁶ "Abba, Father," he said, "everything is possible for you. Take this cup away from me. But not what I want — what you want."

³⁷ He went back and found them sleeping. "Simon!" he said to Peter. "Asleep? Couldn't you stay awake for one hour?

³⁸ Stay awake and pray so that you won't give in when you're tested. Your spirit is willing enough, but your body is weak."

³⁹ He went away again and prayed the same prayer.

⁴⁰ When he came back, he found them sleeping again — they couldn't keep their eyes open. They had no idea what to say to him.

⁴¹ He came back a third time and said, "Still sleeping? Still resting? Enough! The hour has come. Look — the Son of Man is being handed over to sinners.

⁴² Get up! Let's go! Here comes my betrayer!"

⁴³ The words were barely out of his mouth when Judas appeared — one of the twelve — leading a mob armed with swords and clubs, sent by the chief priests, the scholars, and the elders.

⁴⁴ The betrayer had arranged a signal: "The one I kiss — that's him. Grab him and take him away under guard."

⁴⁵ Judas walked straight up to Jesus and said, "Rabbi!" — and kissed him.

⁴⁶ The men seized Jesus and held him.

⁴⁷ One of the bystanders drew his sword and struck the high priest's servant, slicing off his ear.

⁴⁸ Jesus said to the mob, "Am I some kind of rebel that you had to come with swords and clubs to capture me?

⁴⁹ I was right there in the temple every day, teaching. You never laid a hand on me. But this is happening to fulfill what Scripture says."

⁵⁰ Then all the disciples abandoned him and ran.

⁵¹ A young man had been following Jesus, wearing nothing but a linen sheet. They grabbed him,

⁵² but he slipped out of the sheet and ran away naked.

⁵³ They took Jesus to the high priest's residence, where all the chief priests, elders, and scholars had assembled.

⁵⁴ Peter followed at a safe distance, all the way into the high priest's courtyard. He sat with the guards and warmed himself by the fire.

⁵⁵ Meanwhile, the chief priests and the entire council were trying to find evidence against Jesus so they could execute him, but they couldn't find any.

⁵⁶ Many gave false testimony, but their stories didn't match.

⁵⁷ Some stood up and lied about him, saying,

⁵⁸ "We heard him say, 'I will destroy this temple built by human hands, and in three days I'll build another one — not made by human hands.'"

⁵⁹ But even on this point, their stories contradicted each other.

⁶⁰ The high priest stood up before them all and demanded, "Aren't you going to respond? What about this testimony against you?"

⁶¹ Jesus said nothing. He gave no answer. The high priest pressed again: "Are you the Messiah, the Son of the Blessed One?"

⁶² Jesus said, "I am. And you will see the Son of Man sitting at the right hand of the Almighty, coming on the clouds of heaven."

⁶³ The high priest ripped his robes. "Why do we need any more witnesses?" he said.

⁶⁴ "You heard the blasphemy! What's your verdict?" They all condemned him. He deserved death.

⁶⁵ Some of them began spitting on him. They blindfolded him, punched him, and taunted, "Prophesy! Tell us who hit you!" Then the guards took him and beat him.

⁶⁶ Meanwhile, Peter was still down in the courtyard. One of the high priest's servant girls came by.

⁶⁷ She noticed Peter warming himself, looked closely at him, and said, "You were with the Nazarene — Jesus."

⁶⁸ Peter denied it flatly: "I don't know what you're talking about." He moved toward the gateway — and a rooster crowed.

⁶⁹ The servant girl spotted him again and told the people standing around, "This man is one of them."

⁷⁰ Peter denied it again. A little later, the bystanders said to Peter, "You're definitely one of them. You're a Galilean — it's obvious."

⁷¹ Peter began to call down curses on himself. "I swear to God," he said, "I don't know the man you're talking about!"

⁷² Immediately the rooster crowed a second time. Then Peter remembered what Jesus had told him: "Before the rooster crows twice, you will disown me three times." And he broke down and sobbed.

---
## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

1. **The unnamed woman (vv. 3-9):** She is the only person in Mark who truly understands what is about to happen. While the disciples argue about greatness and Peter promises he'll never fail, this woman — unnamed, unspeaking — anoints Jesus for burial. Jesus says her act will be remembered wherever the gospel is preached. Ironically, her name has been forgotten.

2. **"This is my body... this is my blood" (vv. 22-24):** The words that launched two thousand years of eucharistic theology. Mark's account is spare — no command to "do this in remembrance" (that comes from Paul in 1 Corinthians 11:24-25). The "blood of the covenant" echoes Exodus 24:8. "Poured out for many" echoes Isaiah 53:12.

3. **Gethsemane (vv. 32-42):** Mark's Gethsemane is the most raw and human portrayal in the Gospels. Jesus is "deeply distressed and troubled" — the Greek (ἐκθαμβεῖσθαι καὶ ἀδημονεῖν) suggests horror and anguished bewilderment. He begs God to find another way. Luke adds a comforting angel; John has Jesus say "for this purpose I have come." Mark has none of that cushioning.

4. **The naked young man (vv. 51-52):** One of the strangest episodes in the Gospels — unique to Mark. A young man flees naked, leaving behind his linen cloth. His identity is debated endlessly. But the literary function is clear: this is the last follower of Jesus, and even he abandons him. Jesus goes to his trial utterly alone.

5. **"I am" (v. 62):** At the climactic moment, Jesus drops all secrecy. After an entire Gospel of commanding silence about his identity, he now declares it openly — "I am" — before the one audience guaranteed to kill him for it.

6. **Peter's collapse (vv. 66-72):** The triple denial unfolds with devastating precision. Each denial escalates — from confused deflection to flat denial to cursing and swearing. The rooster crows exactly as predicted. Peter remembers. And he breaks.

— PNEUMA, AI Translator
